intro: A public window into one reader's library — not the books, but the spines that run across them: the tensions it holds unresolved, the figures it orbits, the collections hiding in plain sight.

curated.name: Free Will: Eliminated vs. Presupposed
curated.description: Sapolsky argues free will is an incoherent residual category — every behavior traces to biology and prior environment the agent never chose, leaving no causal gap for an uncaused decision. Frankfurt's whole account of love and caring presupposes a will that can be wholehearted or divided, integral or self-defeating — a self that authors its own commitments. They cannot both be right about whether there is anything for a 'will' to do.
books: Behave: The Biology of Humans at Our Best and Worst
curated.evidence: Behave: 'Free Will Is an Incoherent Residual Category... no causal gap where an uncaused free decision could insert itself.' The Reasons of Love: 'Self-Love as the Purest Form of Love... the structural integrity of a will that does not work against itself.'

curated.name: Darwin as gravitational center
curated.description: With 21 descending books — nearly double the next lineage — Darwin is the collection's strongest intellectual ancestor. The convergence is reinforced by see-also clustering around Sean B. Carroll, Stephen Jay Gould, and 'The Hedgehog, the Fox, and the Magister's Pox.' This is a library where evolutionary thinking is the dominant explanatory frame, spilling well beyond the 87 Science books into Business and Economics reasoning by analogy.
curated.evidence: Darwin: 21 books (top lineage); seeAlso convergence on Sean B. Carroll (2), Stephen Jay Gould (2), The Hedgehog/Fox/Magister's Pox (2), Scientific Revolution (2)

curated.name: The Lebanese Probability Monastery
curated.description: A complete, near-canonical Taleb shelf — every major Incerto volume plus the technical 'Dynamic Hedging' and 'Statistical Consequences of Fat Tails.' The collector didn't just read Taleb popularly; they bought the trader's options manual and the academic fat-tails text, signaling a desire to follow the lineage all the way down to its mathematical machinery, not stop at the airport bestseller.
books: Dynamic Hedging: Managing Vanilla and Exotic Options
curated.evidence: All six tagged 'Empirical skepticism & probability theory (Lebanon)'; lineage Taleb(6) ranks 5th overall

curated.name: A graduate seminar in cybernetics hiding in plain sight
curated.description: The owner has assembled the actual dense foundational canon of systems/cybernetics theory — not the popularizations. Cybernetics (Wiener), An Introduction to Cybernetics (Ashby), Steps to an Ecology of Mind (Bateson), and Gödel, Escher, Bach are all marked dense. This is the real primary-source spine of second-order systems thinking, yet the 'systems' register is only 33 books and the accessible systems titles (Thinking in Systems, Emergence, Systemantics) sit on top of it. The owner may not realize they own the hard core, not just the friendly summaries.
books: Steps to an Ecology of Mind
curated.evidence: 4 dense systems-theory primary texts (Wiener, Ashby, Bateson, Hofstadter) coexisting with accessible derivatives like Thinking in Systems, Emergence, Systemantics

curated.name: Growth: Imperative vs. Impossible
curated.description: Cowen argues compounding economic growth is ethically dominant over almost everything else, dwarfing redistribution and short-term concerns. Smil argues all growth follows S-curves bounded by finite energy and material flows, and that perpetual quantitative expansion is a historical anomaly that contradicts the saturation dynamics governing every physical system. One says maximize growth above all; the other says continued material growth is physically impossible.
curated.evidence: Stubborn Attachments: 'Compounding Growth Dwarfs Other Concerns... maximizing long-run growth ethically dominant.' Growth: 'Material Growth Cannot Continue Indefinitely... the post-1800 era of continuous quantitative expansion is a historical anomaly, not a permanent state.'

curated.name: Kahneman and the decision-science axis
curated.description: 10 books trace to Kahneman, and the see-also references to 'Noise: A Flaw in Human Judgment' and 'Groupthink' confirm a behavioral-economics/judgment cluster. This is the intellectual engine behind the large Business (119) and Economics (51) holdings — the owner reads business through the lens of cognitive bias.
curated.evidence: Kahneman: 10 books; seeAlso Noise: A Flaw in Human Judgment (2), Groupthink (2); American organizational psychology tradition (10)

curated.name: Empirical Evolutionists vs. Contemplative Yogis
curated.description: Two coherent register-clusters sit at opposite poles and never speak to each other. The empirical pole is dominated by Darwin/Dawkins evolutionary biology and Kahneman-style cognitive science — cold, mechanism-seeking, deflationary about human nature. The contemplative pole is dominated by Patanjali, the Gita, Zen, and yoga — warm, practice-based, transformative about human nature. The same reader holds both 'The Selfish Gene' and 'The Yoga Sutras of Patanjali' with no bridging texts that reconcile mechanism and meaning.
curated.evidence: empirical:117 concentrated in Science/Psychology; contemplative:41 concentrated in Spirituality/Health; Darwin lineage:21 vs Patanjali lineage:13

curated.name: Markets: Self-Correcting vs. Requiring Stabilization
curated.description: Friedman argues economic freedom precedes political freedom and that government intervention concentrates power dangerously; markets disperse power and discipline themselves. Posner argues capitalism is inherently prone to bubbles and depressions and cannot self-correct in time, making active government stabilization a constitutive part of capitalism rather than an intrusion. A direct clash over whether the market self-regulates or structurally requires the state.
curated.evidence: Free to Choose: 'Regulation Captured by Those Regulated... barriers to entry and protection of incumbents rather than consumers.' A Failure of Capitalism: 'Capitalism Requires Active Government Stabilization... government is a constitutive part of capitalism rather than an outside intruder.'

curated.name: Chicago Libertarian vs. Heterodox Keynesian Economics
curated.description: The collection holds both poles of 20th-century economic combat without resolution: Friedman's 'Free to Choose,' Cowen's market libertarianism, the Sovereign-Individual techno-libertarians — against Stiglitz's 'Globalization and Its Discontents,' Krugman's 'Conscience of a Liberal,' Keynes's General Theory and Sachs. The collector reads the argument, not a side.
books: The General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money
curated.evidence: Chicago School / libertarian economics tags coexist with American heterodox economics, liberal Keynesian, and British Cambridge economics

curated.name: The Science of Collective Error
curated.description: Beneath the surface 'Business' and 'Science' categories sits an unnamed collection about how groups and individuals systematically misjudge reality. The convergence on Groupthink, Noise, and Epidemiology reveals a coherent preoccupation with the mechanics of flawed human judgment — at both crowd scale and cognitive scale — that no single category captures.
books: Extraordinary Popular Delusions and the Madness of Crowds
curated.evidence: Groupthink pointed to by Structure of Scientific Revolutions + Extraordinary Popular Delusions; Noise: A Flaw in Human Judgment pointed to by Signal and the Noise + Thinking Fast and Slow; Epidemiology pointed to by Ghost Map + Tipping Point. Kahneman lineage (10 books) anchors the cognitive side.
